revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Fujiko (religion)]][[ja:富士講]]
{{Use dmy dates|date=January 2024}} {{pp-semi|small=yes}}
'''Fujiko''' (富士講) is a Japanese religious group. One of the popular beliefs established in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, especially in the eastern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kanto|1=Kantō region}} region centered on {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}, with a lineage of [[:en:Kakugyo (Fujiko)|Kakugyo]]. The term "Fujikō" is usually used to refer to the religious system and religious movement in general. The term is also sometimes used to refer to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} and its [[Kami|divine spirits]].
The activities of the Fuji-kō consist of regular events called "ogami" and climbing Fuji. At the Ogami, they read the "Otsutae (Report)," a sutra of devotional service, and perform the "Otakiaage (burning up)" using an assembled altar called the "Ogami Dance (Worship Chest).
There are also examples of {{ill|Fujizuka|en|Fujizuka|qid=Q5507555|lt=Fuji Mounds|1=Fujizuka}} (sometimes natural mountains are used instead) built with stones and earth to worship the god of Mt. Fuji as a place of worship (see the article on {{ill|Fujizuka|en|Fujizuka|qid=Q5507555|lt=Fuji Mound|1=Fujizuka}} for details).
Oshi was the leader of the faith taught by Kakugyo, and at the same time, he was responsible for providing accommodations for the members of the Fuji-kō when they climbed Fuji. During the closing period of the mountain, Goshi would visit Fuji-kō in Edo and other areas to preach the teachings. In the summer, when Mt. Fuji was about to open, Fuji-kō members came one after another to Goshi's homes in Kawaguchi and Yoshida, and he provided them with lodgings, information about the mountain trail, food and equipment necessary for climbing, and various other services.
In the Edo period (1603–1868), "Fuji-kō" in the narrow sense referred only to the activities of masters in Yoshida, and the activities of masters in Kawaguchi, Sushiri, Suyama, and other areas outside Yoshida, as well as the relationship between masters and leaders, were not included in "Fuji-kō," and there is a suggestion that this was the more common form.<ref>高埜利彦『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014th year ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P324</ref>。
In addition to the above, Fuji-kō (Asama-kō) is also a name for a group of people who believe in Fuji, derived from [[Shugendō|Shugendo]]. It is distributed in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chubu region|1=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kinki region|1=Kansai region}}, but the actual situation differs greatly from the above, and is characterized by water practices (Fuji sakurei) held near water in early summer. They also climb Mt. Fuji, but they alternate climbing {{ill|Mount Ōmine|en|Mount Ōmine|qid=Q3695989|lt=Ominesan|1=Mount Ōmine}} every other year, a behavior not seen in the Kanto region.
== History ==
Fuji-kō in the narrow sense originates from a sect of [[:en:Kakugyo (Fujiko)|Kakugyo]] Fujibutsu, who practiced asceticism in [[Sengen shrine|Fuji Shrine]]. Later, Wantoshin (Akaba Shosaemon) and others formed the first kosha, which had the following three rules.<ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=murHqMaXDJ0C&pg=PT130 "Fuji no Kenkyu 3: Fuji no Kyoshin" (Fuji Faith), Sengen Shrine (ed. Inobe Shigeo), Kokin Shoin, 1929] p107</ref>
# If you do good, you are good; if you do bad, you are bad. # If you earn, you will be blessed and noble, and your life will be long without disease. # If you are idle, you will be poor, have sickness, and your life will be short.
After the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|qid=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} period, it was developed by Murakami Mitsukiyo and Shokugyo Miroku. Murakami was supported mainly by the feudal lords and the upper class, while Shokugyo, who preached that diligently working at the family business would save one's life, was enthusiastically supported by the common people of Edo.<ref name="repo1">[http://www.wha.or.jp/images/special/report201307_book.pdf 世界遺産『富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉』江戸時代に流行した民衆信仰「富士講」と日本人本来の心の領域] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140316165706/http://www.wha.or.jp/images/special/report201307_book.pdf|date=16 March 2014}} [[岩下哲典]]、世界遺産アカデミー20号、2013rd year 7月15日</ref>
He was the fifth (or sixth, depending on your point of view) disciple of Kakugyo, and after his death Kakugyo's beliefs did not belong to any existing religious force, so the group that was formed after Shokugyo Miroku's death was an independent religious force. , Suzū, Shizuoka Prefecture). The monuments were originally erected at the Fuji Mound in {{ill|Azabu|en|Azabu|qid=Q793432|lt=Azabu|1=Azabu}}, Edo to commemorate the climb of Fuji.]] On Fuji, Goshi communities were established at each trailhead and conducted missionary activities in the Kanto region and elsewhere, attracting many worshippers to Mt. Fuji. In particular, the Omiya and Suyama mouths, which took a long time to restore after the great eruption of Hōei, were crowded with worshippers from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} and the Kanto region. At the height of the eruption, there were nearly a hundred houses of priests in Yoshida-guchi. In the late Edo period (1603–1868), there were so many societies that it was said that there were 800 societies in Edo and 80,000 members in the societies.
Fuji-kō was often forbidden by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Edo shogunate]] as it was considered undesirable due to its religious policy, but it was not suppressed so severely that it caused deaths.
However, after the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, {{ill|Oppression|en|Oppression|qid=Q252000|lt=suppression|1=Oppression}} from Shinto forces became very severe. As a result, there was no choice. Some of the Fuji-kō were turned into denominational Shintoism, such as Kyōkyō by Fujimichi, who was a follower of Shokugyo, Maruyama-kō by Rokurobei Ito, who was an ascetic, and Fuso-kō by Hanshi Shishino, a follower of [[Hirata Atsutane|Hirata]], who wanted to mobilize the various forces of the Fuji faith into [[:en:State Shintoism|State Shintoism]].
After the Meiji era, and especially after {{ill|Post-war|en|Post-war|qid=Q1201472|lt=Post-war|1=Post-war}}, Mt. Fuji and the surrounding area became a tourist attraction, and climbing Mt. Fuji itself became recognized as a leisure activity, allowing people to climb Mt. Fuji casually, and the Fuji-kō, which sought faith as a motivation for climbing, declined greatly. For example, the construction of the monument tower at the Hitoana Fuji-kou Ruins has not been done since 1964.<ref name="suisenshogennan">[http://www.fujisan-3776.jp/topics/H23.07.27suisenshogennan.pdf 「富士山」推薦書原案] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130525005847/http://www.fujisan-3776.jp/topics/H23.07.27suisenshogennan.pdf |date=25 May 2013 }}、富士山世界文化遺産登録推進両県合同会議、2011.07.27</ref>
The number of Fuji-ko members has decreased drastically, and it is rare to see Fuji-ko members active on the streets of Tokyo. However, you can still see Fuji-ko members making pilgrimages to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}.
As of 2006 (Heisei 18), there are more than a dozen groups active, and three Goshi's houses ({{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|qid=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}}) accept them.<ref>最盛期の軒数と現在の数は、『NHK [[新日本紀行]]ふたたび』 平成18th year 10/14放送より</ref>
== Climbing Mt. Fuji == Fuji is the holy place for the Fuji-kō, and they repeatedly climb Mt. Fuji as a form of pilgrimage. The number of days and manner of climb vary depending on the sect, but a certain period of time is required to purify oneself before climbing the mountain.
The man cave, the site of Kakugyo's ascetic practices, is considered a sacred place and is visited by Buddhist priests. The Hitoana Sengen Shrine, located next to the man cave, has Kakugyo as its main deity. Many monuments and pagodas were erected in the area, and there are still about 230 monuments and pagodas left today. It is now known as the "Hitoana Fuji-kou Ruins.
There was a culture of dedicating a monument called "Fuji Ko monument" to the followers of Fuji Ko. (The monument is characterized by a mark called "kasa-in". (One of the characteristics of this monument is the "kasa-in" mark, which differs from one kosha to another, and includes "Marusan" and "Yamasan".<ref>[http://www.fy-museum.jp/div/tosyo/pdf/marubi/MARUBI25.pdf 博物館だよりMARUBI №25] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120721042315/http://www.fy-museum.jp/div/tosyo/pdf/marubi/MARUBI25.pdf |date=21 July 2012 }}、富士吉田市歴史民俗博物館、（2005.11.30)</ref>）
== Pilgrimage == photographed in 1880.]] print by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|qid=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}} with Fujiko pilgrims]]
Fuji, but also practiced pilgrimage and water asceticism at pilgrimage sites such as {{ill|Fuji Five Lakes|en|Fuji Five Lakes|qid=Q952163|lt=Fuji Five Lakes|1=Fuji Five Lakes}} and {{ill|Shiraito Falls|en|Shiraito Falls|qid=Q38411|lt=Shiraito Falls|1=Shiraito Falls}}.<ref name="suisenshogennan" />
=== Fuji Hakkai === There are pilgrimage sites collectively called '''Fuji Hakkai''', which can be divided into '''Inner Hakkai''' consisting mainly of sacred sites around Fuji. In the Inner Eight Seas, each of the five lakes of Fuji, as well as Lake Akemi (Asumi no Umi, Fujiyoshida City), Lake Shioren (Shibirei no Umi, {{ill|Ichikawamisato|en|Ichikawamisato|qid=Q1204453|lt=Ichikawa Misato Town|1=Ichikawamisato}}), and Sembata (Senzu no Umi, {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|qid=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida City|1=Fujiyoshida}}) were pilgrimage sites for the Fuji-kō in the early modern period. However, before that, Sudo-ko (Lake {{ill|Numazu|en|Numazu|qid=Q241037|lt=Numazu City|1=Numazu}}, {{ill|Fuji, Shizuoka|en|Fuji, Shizuoka|qid=Q328613|lt=Fuji City|1=Fuji, Shizuoka}}) was included in the "Eight Seas of Fuji" instead of Sembata.
The outer eight seas include the Futami Sea (Futamiura, Mie Prefecture), {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|qid=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}} ({{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}}, Shiga Prefecture), {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|qid=Q1206692|lt=Suwa Lake|1=Lake Suwa}} (Nagano Prefecture), {{ill|Lake Haruna|en|Lake Haruna|qid=Q3214922|lt=Haruna Lake|1=Lake Haruna}} (Gunma Prefecture), Nikko Lake ({{ill|Lake Chūzenji|en|Lake Chūzenji|qid=Q117923|lt=Lake Chūzenji|1=Lake Chūzenji}}, Tochigi Prefecture), Lake Sakura (Sakuragaike, Shizuoka Prefecture), Lake Kashima ({{ill|Lake Kasumigaura|en|Lake Kasumigaura|qid=Q1077984|lt=Kasumigaura|1=Lake Kasumigaura}}, Ibaraki Prefecture), and Lake Hakone ({{ill|Lake Ashi|en|Lake Ashi|qid=Q187053|lt=Lake Ashi|1=Lake Ashi}}no, Kanagawa Prefecture).
== See also == * {{ill|Fujizuka|en|Fujizuka|qid=Q5507555|lt=Fujizuka|1=Fujizuka}} * {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|qid=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage|1=Pilgrimage}} * <bdi>[[Sengen shrine]]</bdi>
== Footnotes ==
=== Annotations === {{Notelist}}
=== References === {{Reflist}}
== References == * Koichiro Iwashina, History of the Fuji School, Meishu Shuppan, 1983, ISBN 4626015832. * Shigeo Inobe, The Faith of Fuji (Studies in Fuji, 3, Kokin Shoin, 1928. (Reprinted by Meishu Shuppan in 1973 and 1983.) * Eiji Hirano, The Faith of Fuji Sengen, Yuzankaku, 1987. * {{ill|Miyata Noboru|en|Miyata Noboru|qid=Q6884422|lt=Miyata Noboru|1=Miyata Noboru}}, Sukui no Kami to Ofuji-san, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2006.
== External links == * [https://www.sengenjinja.jp/fujikou/index.html Fujiko and Goshi] – Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine * {{Kotobank|name=富士講|display=富士講}}
[[File:Monks walk through the approach of Northern Fuji Sengen Shrine.JPG|right|thumb|Fuji-ko members on the approach to Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine.]] [[File:The former-Togawa family house.JPG|right|thumb|This is a typical building of the house of the Oshi of the Fujikou sect.]] [[File:Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen jinja 20190826c1.jpg|thumb|Kitaguchi Hongu Fushi Asama Shrine]] [[File:Fujikou.jpg|thumb|A group of monuments commemorating the Fuji-kō at the Higashiguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine (located in {{ill|Oyama, Shizuoka|en|Oyama, Shizuoka|qid=Q726418|lt=Oyama-cho|1=Oyama, Shizuoka}} [[File:Hitoana.jpg|right|thumb|A human hole]] [[File:Fujikouhi.jpg|thumb|Yamasan" mark on a monument of the Fuji-kō (Ningenan Fuji-kō Site)]] [[File:Fuji_Pilgrim_Kusakabe_Kimbei.jpg|thumb|Fuji pilgrim. {{ill|Kusakabe Kimbei|en|Kusakabe Kimbei|qid=Q1794526|lt=Kusakabe Kimbei|1=Kusakabe Kimbei}} [[File:MET JIB18 007.jpg|thumb|[[Ukiyo-e]] [[File:Shiraito water fall 01.jpg|thumb|Shiraito Waterfall]]
